original_abstract: The occurrence of high concentrations of nitrate in various water resources is a significant environmental and human health threat, demanding effective removal technologies. Single atom alloys (SAAs) have emerged as a promising bimetallic material architecture in various thermocatalytic and electrocatalytic schemes including nitrate reduction reaction (NRR). This study suggests that there exists a stark contrast between thermocatalytic (T-NRR) and electrocatalytic (E-NRR) pathways that resulted in dramatic differences in SAA performances. Among Pd/Cu nanoalloys with varying Pd-Cu ratios from 1:100 to 100:1, Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> SAA exhibited the greatest activity (TOF<sub>Pd</sub> = 2 min<sup>-1</sup>) and highest N<sub>2</sub> selectivity (94%) for E-NRR, while the same SAA performed poorly for T-NRR as compared to other nanoalloy counterparts. DFT calculations demonstrate that the improved performance and N<sub>2</sub> selectivity of Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> in E-NRR compared to T-NRR originate from the higher stability of NO<sub>3</sub>* in electrocatalysis and a lower N<sub>2</sub> formation barrier than NH due to localized pH effects and the ability to extract protons from water. This study establishes the performance and mechanistic differences of SAA and nanoalloys for T-NRR versus E-NRR.
humanized_abstract: The presence of nitrate ions in high concentrations in water systems poses a threat to environmental, as well as human health, necessitating the need for efficient removal approaches. Single atom alloys (SAAs) has been identified as a suitable bimetallic material in form of nanoparticles, that work synergistically as catalysts in thermocatalytic and electrocatalytic reduction of nitrate reaction. Interestingly, this study shows that the thermocatalytic (T-NRR) and electrocatalytic (E-NRR) reduction pathways occur through distinct mechanisms. Such mechanisms dictate the activities and selectivities that are exhibited by different SAAs, in this case various Pd different Pd/Cu nanoalloys with varying Pd-Cu ratios from 1:100 up to 100:1 that were synthesized using the ‘one-pot’ method were employed in E-NRR; interestingly enough, the Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> SAA had the highest turnover frequency (TOF<sub>Pd</sub> = 2 min<sup>-1</sup>) and also the highest N<sub>2</sub> selectivity (94 %) while on the other hand the same SAA structure performed poorly towards T-NRR compared to the other nanoalloys, using the same preparation method and structural analysis to verify that SAAs were produced. Density functional theory calculations revealed that the E-NRR of Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> SAA was more stable, giving higher N<sub>2</sub> selectivity than T-NRR due to the presence of NO<sub>3</sub>* intermediate in the E-NRR and lower barrier energy to produce N<sub>2</sub> than that of NH through localized pH and the ability of SAA catalysts to extracted protons from water.
